chunk_summary: Nasogastric (NG) feeding in SAM management ensures adequate intake when oral feeding is unreliable. Guidelines cover indications, placement confirmation, and aspiration prevention, emphasizing safety and practical bedside measures.
learning_objectives: Understand the indications and contraindications for nasogastric feeding in severe acute malnutrition (SAM).
Learn proper techniques for NG tube placement and confirmation, including pH testing and radiographic methods.
Identify strategies to prevent aspiration during NG feeding in pediatric patients.
Recognize criteria for transitioning from NG feeding to oral feeding in SAM management.
key_facts: NG feeding is indicated when oral intake is <80% of the target or in cases of weak suck, unsafe swallow, or inability to finish F-75 volumes.
Contraindications include depressed consciousness, suspected upper GI obstruction, severe facial trauma, and uncontrolled vomiting.
Insertion length is measured using the nose-to-earlobe-to-mid-umbilicus method, and gastric aspirate pH ≤5.5 confirms safe placement.
Re-check tube position after vomiting, retching, or suspected displacement.
Aspiration prevention includes head elevation, avoiding supine feeding, slow bolus delivery, and pausing feeds during respiratory distress.
reference_quote: Use gastric aspirate pH testing when available; feeding is generally considered safe when aspirate pH is ≤5.5. (Source: WHO, Pocket book of hospital care for children)
document_key_topics: Indications for nasogastric feeding in SAM
Techniques for NG tube placement and confirmation
Criteria for transitioning to oral feeding
